questions.stem: In a system design interview, what is the first clarifying move after hearing 'design Twitter'?
options: Propose microservices and Kubernetes before requirements
Ask about scale, features in scope, read/write ratio, and latency SLOs
Refuse until CAP theorem is recited from memory
Immediately draw a sharded Cassandra cluster with Kafka
questions.explanation: Clarify scope and non-functionals first so the design matches real constraints.

questions.stem: Which capacity estimate is most useful early when sizing a feed service?
options: Number of microservices before any traffic estimate
Rough QPS, payload size, storage growth, and peak vs average load
Exact JVM GC pause times for every pod
Brand colors for the mobile app
questions.explanation: Back-of-envelope QPS/storage/growth drives caching, sharding, and cost choices.

questions.stem: Horizontal vs vertical scaling — interview-ready distinction?
options: Vertical always beats horizontal for worldwide latency
They are identical once you use Docker
Horizontal only applies to databases; vertical only to CDNs
Horizontal adds more machines; vertical grows CPU/RAM on one box
questions.explanation: Scale-out adds nodes; scale-up upsizes a single node — different failure and cost trade-offs.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Why put a CDN in front of mostly static asset traffic? Which answer is best?
options: Edge caches cut origin load and improve latency for cacheable content
CDNs encrypt passwords better than bcrypt
CDNs replace databases for all transactional writes
CDNs force strong consistency on every API call
questions.explanation: CDN edge caches static/cacheable content near users and shields origin.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Load balancer Layer 4 vs Layer 7 — crisp difference? Which answer is best?
options: L7 only does UDP; L4 only does GraphQL
L4 terminates TLS and rewrites HTML; L7 cannot
They differ only by cloud vendor logo
L4 routes on IP/port/TCP; L7 can route on HTTP path/host/headers
questions.explanation: L4 is transport-level; L7 understands application protocol for smarter routing.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: When is a reverse proxy especially valuable in a web tier design? Which answer is best?
options: Never — clients should always hit app servers directly
Only for batch MapReduce jobs
For TLS termination, buffering, compression, and routing before app servers
Only when you have no TLS or no logging needs
questions.explanation: Reverse proxies centralize TLS, routing, and request handling in front of apps.

questions.stem: Stateless app servers — why do interviewers prefer them for web tiers?
options: They eliminate the need for databases entirely
Any instance can serve any request, enabling easy horizontal scale and failover
They store all user sessions only in process memory forever
They force sticky sessions for every request
questions.explanation: Statelessness lets the LB spray traffic freely and replace instances safely.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Sticky sessions — what trade-off should you call out? Which answer is best?
options: They are required for every REST API by HTTP standard
They replace the need for auth tokens
They simplify failover and never pin users to one instance
They improve cache locality for a user but hurt failover and balanced scale-out
questions.explanation: Affinity helps local state/cache but couples users to instances and complicates HA.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Cache-aside vs write-through — which statement is accurate? Which answer is best?
options: Cache-aside: app reads cache then DB on miss; write-through updates cache on writes via cache layer
Cache-aside only works for writes, never reads
Write-through never writes to the database
Both forbid TTLs and invalidation
questions.explanation: Cache-aside is app-managed fill; write-through keeps cache updated on write path.

questions.stem: When would you choose Redis as a session/cache store over only DB sessions?
options: When you must never lose data even without persistence or replication
When Redis replaces DNS entirely
When you need lower latency and shared sessions across many app instances
When you want permanent ACID transactional storage for all ledger entries
questions.explanation: Redis shines for fast shared ephemeral state; durability needs careful config.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Cache stampede / thundering herd — best mitigation to mention? Which answer is best?
options: Never cache anything ever again
Singleflight/locking, soft TTLs, probabilistic early refresh, or request coalescing
Use only sticky sessions with no TTLs
Delete all caches every millisecond
questions.explanation: Coalesce refreshes and staggered/soft expiry prevent simultaneous mass rebuilds.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Consistent hashing — why use it for a cache or shard ring? Which answer is best?
options: It sorts results alphabetically for SQL
It guarantees strong consistency across regions automatically
It minimizes key remapping when the ring membership changes
It maximizes remapping when nodes join/leave compared to modulo hashing
questions.explanation: Consistent hashing relocates few keys on membership change vs naïve modulo.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Database sharding — primary reason to introduce it? Which answer is best?
options: Because ORMs require sharding by law
To avoid indexes entirely
To make SQL joins always faster across all shards
When a single DB's storage/CPU/write throughput cannot meet growth
questions.explanation: Shard when one primary cannot hold data or absorb write/read load.

questions.stem: Hot key / hotspot shard — interview mitigation?
options: Ban all secondary indexes
Salting/key splitting, dedicated cache, read replicas, or redesign the key
Only use vertical scaling forever
Ignore it; hashing always perfectly balances skew
questions.explanation: Hotspots need key redesign, caching, or special handling beyond plain sharding.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Leader-follower replication — classic read scaling benefit? Which answer is best?
options: Writes go to followers only; leader is read-only
Reads can fan out to replicas while writes hit the primary
It provides multi-master conflict-free writes by default
It eliminates replication lag completely
questions.explanation: Replicas absorb read traffic; primary owns writes (with lag caveats).

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Replication lag — what product risk should you flag? Which answer is best?
options: Automatic schema migrations without DBA involvement
Stale reads shortly after writes if reading a lagging replica
Impossibility of backups
Guaranteed loss of all committed writes on the primary
questions.explanation: Async replicas may serve stale data after a recent write.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: SQL vs NoSQL — when is a relational DB often the better default? Which answer is best?
options: Only when data is completely unstructured blobs
Only for caching CDN edges
Never, because NoSQL always scales better for every workload
When you need flexible joins, strong transactions, and evolving ad-hoc queries
questions.explanation: Relational DBs excel at structured data, ACID, and complex queries.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: When might a document store be a better fit than rigid SQL schemas? Which answer is best?
options: When you require multi-row serializable joins across arbitrary relations
When you must run only stored procedures forever
When you need flexible nested documents and evolving schemas with simple access patterns
When the only need is POSIX file locking
questions.explanation: Document DBs fit flexible documents and known access paths; joins/transactions vary by product.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Message queue vs direct sync RPC — when prefer the queue? Which answer is best?
options: When TCP is unavailable
When CAP does not apply to distributed systems
When you need the absolute lowest latency synchronous reply always
When producers and consumers need decoupling, buffering, and async retry
questions.explanation: Queues absorb spikes and decouple services for async work.

questions.stem: Idempotency keys on payment APIs — why do interviewers love this topic?
options: They make retries safe so duplicate requests do not double-charge
They compress JSON payloads by 90%
They replace TLS for payment traffic
They force eventual consistency off by default
questions.explanation: Clients retry; idempotency prevents duplicate side effects.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Designing a URL shortener — what is a core storage key design? Which answer is best?
options: Store only the long URL with no short code mapping
Use DNS TXT records as the only datastore
Hash passwords into short codes for security
Map short code → long URL (and often reverse metadata) with unique code generation
questions.explanation: Shorteners need unique short keys mapping to original URLs plus analytics/metadata.

questions.stem: Base62 short codes — main benefit cited in interviews?
options: They are URL-safe compact encodings of numeric IDs
They enforce ACID across regions
They replace HTTPS
They guarantee cryptographic unguessability without rate limits
questions.explanation: Base62 packs IDs into short URL-safe strings; security still needs other controls.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: News feed fan-out on write vs on read — trade-off summary? Which answer is best?
options: Fan-out on write is always cheaper for celebrities with millions of followers
They are identical at all scales
Fan-out on read never needs caching
Fan-out on write precomputes timelines (fast read, heavy write); on read computes at fetch time
questions.explanation: Push vs pull fan-out trades write amplification for read latency (hybrid common).

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Rate limiting algorithms — token bucket vs fixed window? Which answer is best?
options: Token bucket cannot refill over time
Neither can be implemented with Redis
Token bucket allows bursts up to bucket size; fixed window counts per interval with edge bursts
Fixed window never has boundary burst issues
questions.explanation: Token bucket smooths with burst allowance; fixed windows are simple but edge-bursty.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Where should API rate limits usually be enforced? Which answer is best?
options: Only on the client with no server checks
Only inside the database trigger layer
At the edge/gateway or a shared limiter before expensive work
Never for public APIs
questions.explanation: Server-side edge/gateway limits protect backends; clients are not trusted.

questions.stem: Designing a chat system — why separate online presence from message storage?
options: Presence must be strongly consistent across continents always
WebSockets cannot carry chat text
Presence is high-churn ephemeral state; messages need durable ordered storage
Messages should live only in browser memory
questions.explanation: Presence is ephemeral; chat history needs durable, often partitioned, storage.

questions.stem: WebSocket vs long polling for realtime chat — fair interview take?
options: Neither needs auth or heartbeats
WebSockets work only over UDP
Long polling is always lower latency than WebSockets
WebSockets give persistent bidirectional channels; long polling is simpler fallback with higher overhead
questions.explanation: WS is efficient bidirectional; long polling is a compatible but chatty alternative.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Object storage for photos vs DB BLOBs — typical recommendation? Which answer is best?
options: Always put multi-GB videos as SQL rows for joins
Object storage cannot use HTTPS
Store large binaries in object storage; keep metadata/URLs in the DB
Never use CDN for images
questions.explanation: Blob stores + metadata DB + CDN is the standard media pattern.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Designing a notification system — why use a queue/worker pool? Which answer is best?
options: To avoid logging delivery status
To make every notification a synchronous DB lock on the user row
To absorb spikes and retry delivery across email/push/SMS channels asynchronously
Because HTTP cannot send JSON
questions.explanation: Async workers handle channel fan-out, retries, and backpressure.

questions.stem: Exactly-once delivery across distributed systems — honest interview stance?
options: End-to-end exactly-once is hard; prefer at-least-once + idempotent consumers
TCP guarantees exactly-once application semantics always
Kafka alone makes every business action exactly-once without design
Exactly-once is free if you use JSON
questions.explanation: True exactly-once is rare; design for at-least-once plus idempotency.

questions.stem: CAP theorem — practical interview phrasing?
options: CAP only applies to single-node SQLite
Under partition, a system must trade off between consistency and availability goals
P means 'performance' not partition tolerance
You can always have perfect C, A, and P simultaneously in async networks
questions.explanation: Partitions force consistency vs availability choices in distributed data systems.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Strong vs eventual consistency — product example framing? Which answer is best?
options: Bank ledger transfers often want strong consistency; social like counts can be eventual
They differ only by UI font
Strong consistency means no replication ever
Eventual consistency is always illegal in finance and social apps
questions.explanation: Pick consistency by correctness needs; not every feature needs linearizability.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Quorum reads/writes (N/R/W) — what do they buy you? Which answer is best?
options: Tunable consistency by overlapping read/write majorities across replicas
Elimination of all network partitions
Automatic GDPR compliance
Free global low latency with W=1 R=1 always
questions.explanation: Quorums trade latency for stronger consistency across replica sets.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Secondary indexes in a sharded DB — key challenge? Which answer is best?
options: Indexes make sharding unnecessary
They only work on CSV files
Global secondary indexes need their own partitioning/consistency strategy
Secondary indexes forbid primary keys
questions.explanation: Non-primary access paths complicate sharding and consistency.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: CQRS — when is it worth mentioning? Which answer is best?
options: Whenever you have a single CRUD table with light traffic
As a replacement for authentication
When read and write models diverge enough that one schema/path hurts both
Only for CSS styling
questions.explanation: CQRS separates write commands from optimized read models when needed.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Event sourcing — core idea? Which answer is best?
options: Delete all logs nightly for privacy
Store only the latest row and never history
Replace HTTP with SMTP
Persist state as an append-only sequence of domain events, derive views
questions.explanation: Event logs are the source of truth; projections rebuild current state.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: API gateway responsibilities commonly listed? Which answer is best?
options: Owning all database schemas and migrations
AuthN/Z, rate limit, routing, request shaping — not core business logic
Replacing service meshes for east-west only with no edge role
Compiling mobile apps
questions.explanation: Gateways handle cross-cutting edge concerns; domain logic stays in services.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Service discovery — why needed in microservice designs? Which answer is best?
options: Instances come and go; clients/proxies need current healthy endpoints
Hardcoded IPs never change in cloud
DNS TTLs are always zero so discovery is unused
Only monoliths need discovery
questions.explanation: Dynamic membership needs discovery or a mesh/proxy control plane.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Circuit breaker pattern — purpose? Which answer is best?
options: Encrypt traffic between services
Fail fast and shed load when a dependency is unhealthy instead of cascading timeouts
Compress images on the CDN
Shard databases automatically
questions.explanation: Breakers stop hammering failing deps and allow recovery.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Bulkheads in resilient design — analogy meaning? Which answer is best?
options: Merge all databases into one for joins
Put all services in one shared thread pool for efficiency
Disable monitoring to reduce noise
Isolate failure domains (pools/queues/threads) so one fault does not sink all traffic
questions.explanation: Bulkheads limit blast radius via isolation of resources.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Designing a distributed lock — what caveat must you mention? Which answer is best?
options: ZooKeeper cannot be used for coordination
Redis SETNX alone is always perfectly safe for money transfers
Locks need TTLs/fencing tokens; clocks and GC pauses can cause unsafe dual holders
Distributed locks are unnecessary if you use JSON
questions.explanation: Correct locking needs expiry + fencing; naive locks are footguns.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Gossip protocols — typical use in large clusters? Which answer is best?
options: Compiling SQL queries
Replacing all consensus algorithms for bank ledgers
Epidemic-style membership/state dissemination without a single central broadcaster
Terminating TLS at the load balancer
questions.explanation: Gossip spreads membership and metadata scalably with eventual convergence.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Leader election — why do systems need it? Which answer is best?
options: To avoid using any heartbeats or terms
To elect a CDN POP for static CSS
To choose JSON over Protobuf
To pick a single coordinator for writes/coordination when only one active leader is desired
questions.explanation: Election establishes a primary for mutually exclusive coordination roles.

questions.stem: Two-phase commit — main interview criticism?
options: It is identical to eventual consistency gossip
Blocking and availability issues if coordinator/participants stall; poor for high-scale cross-service txns
It only works on a single machine
It never provides atomic commit semantics
questions.explanation: 2PC can block and couples availability; sagas/outboxes often preferred across services.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Saga pattern — what problem does it address? Which answer is best?
options: Replacing all message queues
Long-running cross-service workflows with compensating actions instead of distributed 2PC
Encrypting disks at rest
Making CAP irrelevant
questions.explanation: Sagas coordinate multi-step business flows with compensations.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Outbox pattern — why pair it with DB transactions? Which answer is best?
options: Avoid idempotent consumers
Atomically record state change + outbound event, then publish asynchronously for reliable messaging
Bypass the database for all writes
Delete events before committing business rows
questions.explanation: Outbox keeps DB commit and event intent atomic; a relay publishes later.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Bloom filters — where do they help in large systems? Which answer is best?
options: Strong consistency quorums
Exact counting with no error
Encrypting passwords
Probabilistic set membership tests to skip expensive lookups with possible false positives
questions.explanation: Bloom filters cheaply test 'definitely not present' vs 'maybe present'.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Geospatial indexed search (e.g., nearby drivers) — common approach? Which answer is best?
options: Full table scan of all users worldwide on every request only
Store coordinates as passwords
Geohash/S2 cells + secondary indexes or specialized geo stores, with radius queries
Use only CDN cache keys without indexing
questions.explanation: Cell/geohash indexes make proximity queries feasible at scale.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Multi-region active-active — hardest problem to call out? Which answer is best?
options: Disabling monitoring
Conflict resolution and data consistency under concurrent writes across regions
Buying more domain names
Choosing logo colors
questions.explanation: Concurrent multi-region writes force conflict strategy and consistency design.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Back-pressure in streaming/queue pipelines — why design for it? Which answer is best?
options: It only applies to CSS animations
It removes the need for monitoring
It guarantees exactly-once without idempotency
Slow consumers must signal so producers/buffers do not OOM or drop silently forever
questions.explanation: Back-pressure protects system stability when downstream is slower than upstream.
